SURFACE: Read the full workspace state: dashboards, tabs, widgets, layouts.
Switch the active dashboard or tab before acting.
Discover the widget catalog a backend exposes.
Fetch a widget's parameters before creating it — look before you touch.
Enumerate valid values for a widget parameter.
Read the actual rows behind a widget — the facts a note must cite.
Place a configured widget on the active tab.
Change an existing widget's data arguments in place.
Move or resize a widget to exact grid coordinates.
Remove a widget — and only the right one.
Write a durable note or generated chart onto the dashboard.
List and read the connected data backends.
List, read, and instantiate app templates into live dashboards.
Fetch a workspace skill: a reusable analyst workflow.
Read an MCP resource: the app-builder index, or a skill by URI. New in this release — previously zero scenarios touched the resource surface.
Fetch the server's own MCP prompts: tool-usage guidance and session context. New in this release.
Delegate workstreams to other agents with structured task requests.

issueMeaning.too_many_invalid_calls: The agent exceeded the scenario's budget for malformed or rejected tool calls — wrong tool names, unresolved placeholder arguments, or ignoring ids returned by earlier observations.
issueMeaning.schema_not_called_before_create: The agent created or mutated a widget without first fetching its schema. Discipline check: look before you touch.
issueMeaning.missing_generated_widget: A required note or generated chart was absent, landed on the wrong tab, or its content lacked required facts (ticker, EPS, revenue…).
issueMeaning.missing_widget: A required widget was absent: wrong widget id, wrong backend origin, or wrong data arguments like the ticker symbol.
issueMeaning.unlisted_widget_id: The agent used a widget id it never saw in a list_available_widgets result — in other words, it invented one.
issueMeaning.layout_overlap: Two widgets overlap on the dashboard grid. A human opening this dashboard would see widgets stacked on top of each other.
issueMeaning.missing_tab: A required tab is missing from the dashboard, checked by tab id.
issueMeaning.missing_tool_result: The agent never retrieved required information through a tool — e.g. it wrote a note without actually reading the skill or widget data it was asked to use.
issueMeaning.missing_tool_call: A required tool was never invoked — e.g. a delegation scenario where assign_tasks_to_agents never happened.
issueMeaning.layout_mismatch: A widget landed at the wrong grid coordinates or size — the layout the prompt asked for was not produced exactly.

swe.title: SWE-bench
swe.text: The agent receives a software issue and a repository. The benchmark checks whether the patch passes tests. The lesson for WorkspaceBench is the contract: same task, same verifier, comparable result.

terminal.title: Terminal-Bench
terminal.text: The agent completes work through a terminal. The grader cares about final files, logs, and command results, not whether the answer sounded convincing.

webarena.title: WebArena
webarena.text: The agent operates websites through a browser. The lesson for WorkspaceBench is that UI and tool workflows need task-specific verification.

workspace.title: WorkspaceBench
workspace.text: The agent operates OpenBB Workspace through MCP-style tools. The benchmark checks durable workspace state: dashboards, tabs, widgets, layouts, and generated notes.

scenario.title: Scenario
scenario.text: The scenario is the contract. It says what the agent is asked to do, what state exists at the start, which tools are allowed, and what final state counts as success. It also carries metadata for slicing: capability, workflow, domain, subdomain, difficulty.

seed.title: Seed
seed.text: The harness registers fixture backends (Bench Equities, Bench Macro, Bench Portfolio, Bench Stark Enterprise) and seeds deterministic dashboard state. No live market data, no drift: the same task is byte-identical across runs.

discover.title: Discover
discover.text: The agent should inspect available widgets, schemas, and parameter options before acting. This tests whether the tool surface is discoverable and whether the model avoids invented widget ids — a real failure mode the grader tracks.

act.title: Act
act.text: The agent mutates workspace state through tool calls: create a widget, update data args, add a tab, resize layout, instantiate an app template, write a note, or repair a broken dashboard. In interactive mode it sees each tool result before choosing the next call.

snapshot.title: Snapshot
snapshot.text: After the agent acts, the benchmark reads durable workspace state. This is the artifact a human would actually inherit: dashboards, tabs, widgets, layouts, generated notes.

grade.title: Grade
grade.text: The grader checks final state first, then trace discipline: invalid calls, schema-before-create, invented ids, repeated snapshots. It returns pass/fail, a partial score, and stable issue codes that make failures debuggable.

l0.title: L0: Inspect and answer
l0.text: The agent reads an existing dashboard and records what it found as a durable note. Sounds trivial, but it already tests whether the model grounds itself in actual workspace state instead of generic finance knowledge.

l1.title: L1: Single-widget operations
l1.text: Create, update, read, delete, or lay out one widget. This is where schema discovery discipline gets tested: list available widgets, fetch the schema, then create — in that order.

l2.title: L2: Dashboard construction
l2.text: Build a multi-widget dashboard from analyst requirements: right widgets, right tabs, right data arguments, valid layout with no overlaps. This is where composition pressure begins, and mid/small models start dropping sharply at t2+.

l3.title: L3: Apps, skills, and delegation
l3.text: Use app templates, parameter groups, workspace skills through get_skill_content, or task delegation through assign_tasks_to_agents. These test the richer MCP surface beyond widget CRUD.

l4.title: L4: Repair
l4.text: The workspace starts wrong: a stale ticker, a duplicate widget, overlapping layout, a missing tab. The agent must inspect current state, diagnose, and fix it without destroying what already works.

scenario.text: This is the real gen_t1_create_price_performance_msft scenario, abbreviated. Note the trace checks: the grader cares how the agent worked, not only what it produced.

toolcall.text: External agents do not need to import the benchmark. They write JSONL tool calls — one JSON object per line — that the harness executes and grades. This is the reference path for gen_t1_create_price_performance_msft.

command.text: The same harness runs oracle, noop, local model adapters, hosted model adapters, or any custom process that writes tool calls. Context arrives through environment variables like WORKSPACE_BENCH_TASK_JSON.
command.code:
uv run --extra dev workspace-bench run-agent-command \
  --scenario gen_t1_create_price_performance_msft \
  --agent-command "python examples/openai_gpt4_1.py" \
  --run-dir runs/gen_t1_create_price_performance_msft \
  --json

# or compare models interactively, with repeats:
uv run --extra dev workspace-bench compare-models \
  --pack all --difficulty all \
  --repeats 3 --metric pass-at-k

result.text: The result is not a vibe check. It is a structured scorecard: strict pass/fail, partial score (fraction of checks passed), and stable issue codes.

CALIB.label: GPT-5.5
CALIB.note: Frontier compression: composition (t4) barely slows it down; what remains are precision failures (exact layout coordinates) and ambient-state reasoning (the inspect family). This is the headroom warning, quantified.

CALIB.label: Claude Sonnet 5
CALIB.note: Run at temperature 0 via OpenRouter after a structured-output incompatibility was diagnosed and fixed in the harness (OpenRouter can route Anthropic models through Bedrock, where json_schema response_format degrades them to schema-minimal outputs).

CALIB.label: GLM-5.2
CALIB.note: Strong mid-frontier. Its t0 dip is a discipline trait — invalid tool calls and skipped prompt fetches on tasks it treats as beneath deliberation — not a ladder fault.

CALIB.label: gpt-4.1-mini
CALIB.role: gating model · tier bands defined here
CALIB.note: The gating model. Strictly monotonic — this curve defines the tier bands. Its #1 issue is schema-before-create discipline, the exact behavior tool-surface fixes should target.

CALIB.label: gpt-oss:20b
CALIB.note: Open-weights mid model. Independently reproduces the monotonic ladder — the cross-validation that the tier structure is real, not tuned to one vendor.

CALIB.label: Qwen3 8B
CALIB.note: The small-model floor: what an 8B can and cannot do against a real tool surface. Every failure here is headroom for distillation and fine-tuning.

rows.group: by tool family · 20 scenarios each · easiest to hardest
